title: Updating Quotes with Information
subtitle: Bayesian-style fair value when clues appear
intro: Trading games rarely reveal everything at once. A dice cup is lifted halfway; a card is shown then hidden; a coin flip result is announced for one of two coins. Each clue updates fair value — and your quote must move immediately or someone will trade against stale prices.

Conditional expectation is the tool: fair value equals the average outcome given what you know now, not what you knew at the start. If one die shows 4, the expected sum of two dice is 4 plus E[other die] = 4 + 3.5 = 7.5.

Good market makers treat new information as mandatory repricing. They also respect what the room has learned — if everyone saw the same clue, your edge is only in speed and spread, not in secret knowledge.
This lesson practices fair value updates under partial information — a staple of prop-firm interview games.

questions.question: Two fair dice; contract pays the sum. One die is revealed as 3 (the other is hidden). Updated fair value:

explanations.A: Three is only one die, not the sum.
explanations.B: Six ignores the hidden die's expectation.
explanations.D: Nine would require the hidden die to show six with certainty.

remediation.question: One die shows 5. Expected sum of two dice:

explanations.D: Max sum if other is 6, not EV.

remediation.question: Hidden fair die contributes how much to expected sum?

questions.question: Two fair coins (0 or 1 each). Payoff = sum. First coin flipped: 1. Second still hidden. Fair value:

explanations.B: Only first coin, ignoring second's 0.5 EV.
explanations.D: Two is max sum, not EV.

remediation.question: First coin 0, second hidden fair. Expected sum:
Before any flip, expected sum of two fair 0/1 coins:

questions.question: Fair was 7 on two dice. One die shows 6. You still quote centered at 7. A smart trader will:
options: Lift your ask — true fair is now 9.5
Hit your bid — true fair is now 9.5
Not trade — your quote is fine

explanations.A: Correct. New fair = 6 + 3.5 = 9.5; your ask near 7–8 is cheap — they buy.
explanations.B: They'd hit bid if your bid were above fair, not below.

remediation.question: After information, first action is usually:
Die shows 1. Two-dice sum fair value:

questions.question: One card drawn from a standard 52-card deck. Payoff = rank value where Jack=11, Queen=12, King=13, Ace=1, number cards face value. Card is revealed: Ace. Fair value now:

explanations.A: Correct. Card is known — payoff is 1 with certainty.
explanations.C: Eight might confuse with expected before reveal.

remediation.question: Once a random outcome is fully revealed, fair value equals:

explanations.B: Conditional on full info, EV equals outcome.

questions.question: Two dice sum game. You learn the sum is at least 10 (not the exact sum). Which fair value adjustment is most reasonable?
options: Fair stays 7 — no exact sum known
Fair jumps above 7 — average sum conditional on ≥10 exceeds 7

explanations.A: Partial info still updates the distribution — low sums are ruled out.
explanations.B: Correct. Conditioning on sum ≥10 raises expected value well above 7.
explanations.C: At least 10 does not mean exactly 10.

remediation.question: Learning a lower bound on payoff generally:
Sum of two dice is at most 4. Conditional fair is:

questions.question: Public clue moves fair from 5 to 8. You update in 2 seconds; another MM updates instantly. Who gets picked off?
options: You — stale quote sits at old fair longer
The fast MM — speed is bad

explanations.A: Correct. Slow repricing leaves mispriced quotes in the market.
explanations.D: Speed matters when info is public.

remediation.question: When everyone sees the same clue:
options: Quick fair-value math and quote update is critical

explanations.D: That would be fresh and correct.
